articles.title: Elon Musk Admits He Lied to Tesla Customers About Full Self-Driving
articles.summary: Elon Musk has admitted that he knowingly misled Tesla customers for years about the capabilities of Tesla's Full Self-Driving feature. Musk told buyers the technology was essentially ready and that their cars would become fully autonomous, charging them up to $15,000 for a feature that never delivered what he promised. The admission is a stunning moment because Musk made these claims directly to customers' faces while Tesla was actively selling Full Self-Driving as a near-complete product.
articles.content:
For years, Elon Musk stood in front of audiences, appeared on stage, and posted online telling Tesla owners that Full Self-Driving was right around the corner. He said their cars would soon drive themselves completely without human input. He said Tesla owners who bought Full Self-Driving would be able to make money by renting their cars out as robotaxis. Customers paid up to $15,000 based on those promises. None of it came true on the timeline Musk described, and now Musk has admitted he knew it.

Full Self-Driving is Tesla's advanced driver assistance package. Despite the name, it has never made a Tesla fully autonomous. It still requires a human driver to pay attention and take over at any moment. Regulators, safety researchers, and critics pointed this out for years. Tesla and Musk pushed back, repeatedly framing Full Self-Driving as a product that was nearly finished and would reach true autonomy very soon. That messaging continued while Tesla collected billions in revenue from customers who bought the feature.

The admission matters enormously because it confirms what many critics suspected. This was not simply over-optimistic forecasting from an entrepreneur who believed in his product. Musk is now acknowledging that the gap between what he told customers and what Tesla's technology could actually do was something he was aware of. That is a meaningful legal and ethical distinction, and it lands at a time when Tesla is already facing scrutiny over Full Self-Driving related crashes and regulatory investigations.

For Tesla owners who paid for Full Self-Driving, this admission stings. Many of them made financial decisions, including purchasing a Tesla in the first place, based on Musk's promises. Class action lawsuits around Tesla's self-driving claims have been building for several years, and this admission is likely to pour fuel on that fire.

The broader cultural story here is about accountability in the tech industry. Musk is one of the most prominent figures in the world, and the idea that a CEO can spend years making false promises to paying customers and face limited consequences has always been controversial. Whether this admission actually leads to meaningful legal or financial consequences for Musk or Tesla will be the story to watch from here.
keyPoints: Elon Musk has admitted he knowingly misled Tesla customers about Full Self-Driving capabilities
Tesla charged customers up to $15,000 for Full Self-Driving, which never delivered true autonomy
Musk repeatedly promised full autonomy was imminent despite knowing the technology could not deliver it
The admission could fuel existing class action lawsuits against Tesla over self-driving claims
Tesla's Full Self-Driving still requires a human driver to remain alert and in control
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Tesla Full Self-Driving controversy Elon Musk

articles.title: Elon Musk vs Sam Altman Trial Starts April 27, May Expose OpenAI Secrets
articles.summary: A trial between Elon Musk and Sam Altman is scheduled to begin on April 27 in Oakland, California, stemming from Musk's lawsuit against OpenAI, the AI company he helped found before leaving in 2018. Musk claims OpenAI has abandoned its original nonprofit mission to benefit humanity and is instead operating as a for-profit business that enriches Altman and Microsoft. The courtroom drama could pull back the curtain on some of the messiest behind-the-scenes power struggles in tech history.
articles.content:
The tech world's biggest grudge match is about to go to court. Elon Musk and Sam Altman are heading to trial on April 27 in Oakland, California, and the details that come out could reshape how the public sees both men and the company at the centre of the AI revolution, OpenAI.

Here is the backstory. Musk was one of the original co-founders of OpenAI back in 2015, helping set it up as a nonprofit with a mission to develop AI for the benefit of all humanity. He departed from the OpenAI board in 2018, with reports at the time suggesting he wanted to take control of the company as CEO and was turned down. Sam Altman ended up leading OpenAI, which has since grown into one of the most powerful and valuable tech companies on the planet, largely on the back of ChatGPT.

Musk filed his lawsuit arguing that OpenAI has effectively abandoned its founding nonprofit mission by deepening its financial ties with Microsoft and operating more like a for-profit startup than a public-benefit organisation. OpenAI and Altman have pushed back, with Altman suggesting Musk's real motivation is competitive, given that Musk has his own AI company called xAI and its chatbot Grok going up against ChatGPT.

What makes the trial genuinely exciting, in a messy, popcorn-worthy way, is the discovery process. Both sides have had to hand over internal documents, emails, and communications. That means the trial could surface real conversations about who said what during OpenAI's early days, what promises were made, and exactly why Musk left. These are things both sides have had every reason to keep quiet until now.

The broader cultural stakes are high too. OpenAI sits at the centre of the biggest technological shift happening right now. Any revelations about how it is actually run, who holds power, and whether its public-benefit mission was ever real will matter far beyond the legal outcome. People who use ChatGPT every day have a genuine interest in knowing whether the company behind it is what it says it is.

No matter who wins in Oakland, the trial is going to produce a lot of uncomfortable moments for powerful people. Musk and Altman are two of the most influential figures in tech, and watching their competing versions of history get tested under oath is exactly the kind of accountability moment that rarely happens in Silicon Valley.
keyPoints: The Elon Musk vs Sam Altman trial starts April 27 in Oakland, California
Musk co-founded OpenAI in 2015 and left the board in 2018 after reportedly being denied the CEO role
Musk's lawsuit claims OpenAI has abandoned its nonprofit mission in favour of profit, largely through ties with Microsoft
Internal documents and communications could be revealed during the trial
Altman has suggested Musk's lawsuit is motivated by competition from his own AI company xAI and its chatbot Grok

articles.title: US Soldier Used Classified Maduro Intel to Win $400K on Polymarket
articles.summary: A U.S. special forces soldier has been charged with using classified military intelligence about a mission to capture Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro to place a winning bet on Polymarket, the online prediction market. The soldier reportedly walked away with more than $400,000. It is one of the most brazen known cases of someone using secret government intel to profit on a prediction platform.
articles.content:
This one is wild. Federal officials have charged a U.S. special forces soldier with using classified information about a military operation targeting Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro to win more than $400,000 on Polymarket, the online prediction market where users bet real money on real-world events.

Polymarket is a platform where anyone can wager on the outcome of news events, from elections to sports to geopolitical crises. The idea is that the collective wisdom of bettors will predict outcomes accurately. What it is not supposed to be is a tool for people with top-secret access to print money using information the public cannot see.

That is essentially what prosecutors say happened here. The soldier allegedly knew details of the mission to capture Maduro before anyone else did, and used that insider knowledge to place a bet that paid out big. The charges represent a serious breach of military protocol and raise uncomfortable questions about how prediction markets could be exploited by people with access to classified intelligence.

This story matters beyond the legal drama. Polymarket has grown into a legitimate force in how people track and interpret world events, with millions of dollars flowing through it around major news cycles. If insiders with classified access can game it, that undermines the entire premise of the platform. Expect this case to trigger a much bigger conversation about the regulation of prediction markets and the security risks of letting them operate alongside sensitive geopolitical operations.
keyPoints: A U.S. special forces soldier is charged with leaking classified military intel for personal profit
The soldier allegedly used inside knowledge of a mission to capture Nicolás Maduro to bet on Polymarket
The winnings totalled more than $400,000
The case raises serious questions about whether prediction markets like Polymarket can be exploited by people with government access
This could accelerate calls for tighter regulation of online prediction markets

articles.title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.5, Moving Closer to Building One AI Super App
articles.summary: OpenAI has launched GPT-5.5, its latest AI model, which the company says delivers stronger performance across a wide range of tasks. The release is part of OpenAI's broader push toward building what it describes as an AI super app, a single tool that handles almost everything a user might need. GPT-5.5 sits between the existing GPT-5 and whatever comes next, and signals that OpenAI is releasing updates faster than ever.
articles.content:
OpenAI dropped GPT-5.5 today, April 24, 2026, and the message behind the release is almost as interesting as the model itself. Yes, GPT-5.5 brings increased capabilities across a broad variety of categories, according to OpenAI. But the bigger story is what OpenAI says it is building toward: an AI super app.

An AI super app would be a single product that folds in everything. Think chat, image generation, coding help, research, scheduling, shopping and more, all inside one OpenAI-branded experience. Right now those things are scattered across different tools and platforms. OpenAI wants to be the one place you go for all of it, similar to how WeChat works in China as an all-in-one platform for communication, payments and services.

GPT-5.5 is a stepping stone toward that vision. It slots in between GPT-5, which OpenAI released earlier, and the next major model. Incremental releases like this used to be less common for OpenAI, but the company has clearly shifted into a faster release cadence as competition with Google, Meta and Anthropic heats up. Every new model is a chance to demonstrate progress and keep users inside the OpenAI ecosystem.

The practical question is whether GPT-5.5 is meaningfully better for everyday users or whether this is more of a signal release, something designed to show the world that OpenAI is moving fast. OpenAI says the improvements are broad rather than narrow, which suggests the gains are spread across text, reasoning and other capabilities rather than one single breakout feature. If the super app ambition is real, GPT-5.5 is OpenAI quietly laying more of the foundation.
keyPoints: OpenAI has released GPT-5.5, a new model with improved capabilities across multiple categories
OpenAI says GPT-5.5 brings it closer to launching an AI super app, a single tool for nearly all user needs
The super app concept is modelled on platforms like WeChat, combining many functions in one place
GPT-5.5 sits between the earlier GPT-5 model and OpenAI's next major release
The release reflects OpenAI's faster update cadence as competition with Google, Meta and Anthropic intensifies

articles.title: Scientists Finally Solved the Pacific Ocean's Mysterious Golden Orb
articles.summary: Scientists have identified the strange golden orb discovered on the floor of the Pacific Ocean, an object that had puzzled researchers since it was first spotted by a deep sea remotely operated vehicle. The investigation required multiple specialists working together, with researchers calling it "a special case that required focused efforts and expertise of several different individuals." The answer turned out to be biological, not geological.
articles.content:
A few years back, deep sea explorers using a remotely operated vehicle captured footage of a mysterious golden orb sitting on the Pacific Ocean floor. It was smooth, roughly the size of a fist, and glowing with a warm amber color against the dark seafloor. Nobody could immediately tell what it was. Was it a mineral formation? An egg of some kind? Something entirely new? The footage spread widely because the ocean is full of unexplained things, but this one was particularly striking.

Scientists have now confirmed what the golden orb actually was: the egg casing or embryonic housing of an unknown species of marine animal, most likely a type of deep sea fish or invertebrate. The investigation was not quick or simple. Researchers described it as "a special case that required focused efforts and expertise of several different individuals," which is scientist speak for: this one genuinely stumped people for a while and took a team effort to crack.

The deep ocean is one of the least explored environments on Earth. More of the moon's surface has been mapped than the floor of our own oceans, and new species are discovered in deep sea surveys on a fairly regular basis. The Pacific in particular has enormous unexplored trenches and plains where life has adapted to crushing pressure, total darkness, and near freezing temperatures. Finding an egg casing from what might be an undescribed species is consistent with just how much is still unknown down there.

What makes this story satisfying is that it actually got solved. So many deep sea mysteries end with a shrug and a "we are still studying it." In this case, the scientific community kept digging, pulled in the right experts, and landed on a real answer. The golden orb was biological, not geological. It was life, not rock. And it almost certainly came from something living in those depths that we still do not fully understand.

It is a good reminder that you do not have to look to space to find genuinely alien landscapes. The Pacific Ocean floor is strange enough, and we are still only scratching the surface of what lives there.
keyPoints: The mysterious golden orb found on the Pacific Ocean floor has now been identified by scientists
It was determined to be biological in nature, most likely an egg casing from an unknown deep sea species
The investigation required multiple specialists and was described as a uniquely challenging case
The deep ocean remains one of Earth's least explored environments, with new species still being discovered
The finding highlights how much unknown life still exists in the deep Pacific
articles.wikiSearchQuery: deep sea exploration Pacific Ocean unknown species

articles.title: Madonna Is Taking Over Grindr to Promote Confessions II
articles.summary: Madonna is heading to Grindr to promote her upcoming album Confessions II, running an exclusive campaign on the dating and social app for gay and bisexual men. As part of the activation, Madonna is also giving out limited-edition vinyl to fans who engage with the experience. It is a bold, community-first move that plants Confessions II firmly in the queer cultural spaces that have always been Madonna's home base.
articles.content:
Madonna is not going to sit around waiting for the algorithm to do its thing. She is taking Confessions II directly to Grindr, the dating and social app used by millions of gay and bisexual men worldwide, for an exclusive campaign unlike anything the platform has hosted before.

The original Confessions on a Dance Floor came out in 2005 and is widely considered one of Madonna's best albums, a non-stop disco-influenced record that became an anthem across gay clubs around the world. Confessions II is the long-awaited follow-up, and Madonna is leaning all the way into the community that made the first album a phenomenon.

As part of the Grindr takeover, Madonna is offering exclusive limited-edition vinyl to fans. The specifics of how fans can get their hands on the vinyl are tied to how they engage with the campaign on Grindr, making this an interactive experience rather than a standard ad placement. It is the kind of thing that gets people talking and sharing, which is exactly the point.

Choosing Grindr as a promotional partner is a genuinely interesting strategic call. Most major artists promote through Instagram, TikTok, or Spotify. Madonna is skipping the generic rollout and going straight to a specific community with a deep personal connection to her music. It signals that Confessions II is being positioned as a record for her most loyal audience, not just a mainstream pop release chasing chart numbers.

This is also a moment worth noticing for what it says about where music marketing is heading. As mainstream social platforms get noisier, targeted community takeovers like this one feel more intimate and more effective. If Confessions II hits anywhere near as hard as the original, this Grindr campaign will probably be remembered as the moment the conversation started.
keyPoints: Madonna is running an exclusive promotional campaign for Confessions II on Grindr
Fans who engage with the Grindr campaign can receive limited-edition Confessions II vinyl
Confessions II is the follow-up to the 2005 classic Confessions on a Dance Floor, one of Madonna's most celebrated albums
The campaign targets the queer community that has historically been Madonna's most passionate fanbase
The move signals a shift toward intimate community-based music marketing over broad social media rollouts
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Madonna Confessions on a Dance Floor album

articles.title: Tinder Now Makes Users Scan Their Eyes to Prove They Are Human
articles.summary: Tinder is rolling out eye-scanning technology to verify that its users are real people and not AI-generated profiles. The move comes as dating apps across the industry are being flooded with fake, AI-built accounts. It is one of the most aggressive anti-AI identity checks any major social platform has attempted.
articles.content:
Tinder has a fake profile problem and it is getting worse. AI-generated accounts have been pouring into dating apps, and Tinder has decided the only way to fight back is to look users directly in the eye. Literally. The app is now rolling out an eye-scanning verification system that asks users to scan their eyes as proof they are a real, living human being.

The feature works as a biometric check. Tinder users will be prompted to complete the eye scan, which confirms a biological person is behind the profile. If you cannot pass the scan, you cannot be verified. The idea is that no matter how convincing an AI-generated profile looks, it cannot produce a genuine human eye scan on demand.

This is a big swing. Tinder is one of the most downloaded dating apps in the world, and adding biometric verification is a major shift in how these platforms handle identity. For years, dating apps relied on photo uploads and phone number checks. Neither of those stops AI profiles anymore, because AI can generate photorealistic faces and fake numbers with ease.

The move will almost certainly spark a debate about privacy. Handing over biometric eye data to Tinder, a Match Group product, is a much bigger ask than uploading a selfie. But with AI-generated catfishing becoming a genuine crisis on dating platforms, Tinder is betting that users will accept the trade-off to know the person they are swiping on is actually real.
keyPoints: Tinder is introducing eye-scanning verification to fight AI-generated fake profiles
Users must scan their eyes as proof they are a real human being
AI profiles have been flooding dating apps and traditional checks like photo uploads no longer stop them
The move raises significant privacy questions about handing biometric data to a dating platform
It is one of the most aggressive identity verification steps any major social app has taken

articles.title: YouTuber Builds $2,260 Backpack That Makes Him Invisible to 82 Million Cameras
articles.summary: A YouTuber spent $2,260 building an anti-surveillance backpack designed to fool America's 82 million surveillance cameras, and it actually worked. The project is a wild reminder of just how many cameras are watching you on any given day. It is part of a growing wave of DIY privacy tech that is moving from niche hacker circles into mainstream conversation.
articles.content:
America has 82 million surveillance cameras. That is more cameras per person than almost any country on earth. One YouTuber looked at that number and decided to do something about it, spending $2,260 to build a backpack that could make him invisible to all of them.

The build is not magic. It uses a combination of infrared LEDs, reflective materials, and signal-disrupting tech to confuse camera sensors. Surveillance cameras rely on contrast and light to identify shapes and faces. Flood the frame with the right kind of infrared light and the image the camera captures turns into a washed-out blur. The result is that the person wearing the backpack registers as noise rather than a recognisable human being.

The YouTuber tested it in real-world conditions and it worked. Cameras that would normally clock a pedestrian walking past simply could not lock onto a clean image. That is a genuinely impressive result for a device built outside of any lab, with parts that anyone can order online.

This story matters beyond the cool-factor. The United States has more surveillance infrastructure than most people realise, and almost no federal law governs how that footage is stored, shared, or used. A single person with $2,260 and a YouTube channel just demonstrated that the whole system has a meaningful blind spot. Expect this video to get a lot of views, and expect a lot of copycats to follow.
keyPoints: A YouTuber built a working anti-surveillance backpack for $2,260
The US has 82 million surveillance cameras with very little federal regulation
The backpack uses infrared LEDs and reflective materials to blind camera sensors
It was tested in real-world conditions and successfully defeated camera detection
The project highlights a major gap in how surveillance tech can be countered by everyday people

articles.title: BTS Launches Walmart-Exclusive Food Brand Arih With Noodles and Energy Drinks
articles.summary: Fresh off a four-year hiatus, BTS has entered the celebrity business world with Arih, a new food and drink brand sold exclusively at Walmart. The line includes noodles, energy drinks, and sodas, putting BTS alongside a growing wave of celebrities turning their fan bases into consumer brands. With BTS's fanbase ARMY being one of the most dedicated in the world, Arih has a ready-made customer base from day one.
articles.content:
BTS is back, and they brought snacks. The K-pop group, who took a hiatus starting in 2022 while several members completed mandatory South Korean military service, has officially launched Arih, a food and drink brand sold exclusively at Walmart. The line covers noodles, energy drinks, and sodas, landing BTS firmly in the middle of a celebrity consumer goods boom.

The timing makes a lot of sense. BTS spent years building one of the most passionate fan communities on the planet, known as ARMY, and those fans have shown they will show up for anything the group puts their name on. Walmart exclusivity gives Arih instant national shelf space across thousands of stores, which is the kind of distribution most food startups spend years chasing.

BTS joins a long list of artists and athletes who have moved from entertainment into consumer products. Think Ryan Reynolds with Aviation Gin or Dwayne Johnson with Teremana Tequila. But BTS brings something different to the table. Their fanbase skews younger, is fiercely loyal, and has a track record of driving massive numbers when motivated, whether that is streaming records or album sales.

The big question now is whether Arih can hold on past the initial hype wave. First purchases driven by fan love are great, but repeat buyers need to actually enjoy the noodles and drinks. If the products deliver, BTS will have built something with genuine staying power well beyond the pop culture moment.
keyPoints: BTS launched a food and drink brand called Arih, sold exclusively at Walmart
The Arih line includes noodles, energy drinks, and sodas
BTS returned from a four-year hiatus while members completed South Korean military service
The launch puts BTS alongside celebrities like Ryan Reynolds and Dwayne Johnson in the consumer goods space
Walmart exclusivity gives Arih immediate national distribution across thousands of stores

articles.title: NASA Built a Full Typeface From Landsat Satellite Images of Earth
articles.summary: NASA's Kennedy Space Center created an entire interactive typeface made from Landsat satellite images of Earth, released in honor of Earth Day 2026. Each letter of the alphabet is formed by real landscapes, coastlines, and terrain patterns photographed from space. It is a genuinely stunning collision of science communication and design.
articles.content:
NASA's Kennedy Space Center just did something quietly brilliant. To mark Earth Day 2026, the team released an interactive digital tool that lets you type words and see each letter rendered in actual Landsat satellite imagery of Earth. Rivers, deserts, ice sheets, and coastlines all twisted and cropped into the shapes of the alphabet. It is the kind of project that sounds gimmicky until you actually see it, and then you cannot look away.

Landsat is a joint NASA and US Geological Survey program that has been photographing Earth from orbit since 1972, making it one of the longest running Earth observation programs in history. The imagery it captures is used by scientists to track deforestation, glacier melt, urban growth, and natural disasters. Turning that data into a typeface is a clever way to make people actually stop and engage with what those satellites are seeing.

The Kennedy Space Center has been leaning into creative science communication more and more, and this project fits that direction perfectly. Giving someone a tool to write their own name in satellite imagery is a much more visceral way to connect people to Earth observation than publishing a report or posting a statistic. You type "EARTH" and suddenly you are staring at real landscapes from space, arranged letter by letter.

The interactive tool was shared publicly and is designed to be played with, shared, and passed around. That social dimension is clearly intentional. NASA knows that Earth Day content floods every feed on April 22nd, and making something genuinely interactive and beautiful is how you cut through. This one landed. Expect to see a lot of people's names spelled out in satellite imagery making the rounds on social media this week.

It also quietly makes a point. Every letter in that typeface is a real place on Earth that has been photographed, monitored, and in many cases changed dramatically over decades. The typeface is pretty, but underneath it is a reminder of why NASA keeps those Landsat satellites running in the first place.
keyPoints: NASA's Kennedy Space Center built an interactive typeface using real Landsat satellite images of Earth
Each letter is formed by actual landscapes, coastlines, and terrain photographed from orbit
The project was released as an Earth Day 2026 initiative
Landsat is a NASA and US Geological Survey program that has monitored Earth from space since 1972
The tool is designed to be shared interactively so users can type their own words in satellite imagery

articles.title: LEGO Reveals First-Ever Shrek Sets for the Franchise's 25th Anniversary
articles.summary: The LEGO Group has partnered with Universal Products and Experiences to launch the very first Shrek LEGO sets, released to celebrate 25 years of the beloved animated franchise. The launch includes two distinct products aimed at different audiences. Shrek fans who grew up with the films in the early 2000s are now firmly in the adult collector demographic, and LEGO is clearly coming for their wallets.
articles.content:
Far Far Away is coming to your shelf. The LEGO Group has officially unveiled the first-ever Shrek LEGO sets, created in partnership with Universal Products and Experiences to mark the 25th anniversary of the Shrek franchise.

Shrek first hit cinemas in 2001 and became one of the most successful animated film series ever made, spawning four films, a spin-off franchise in Puss in Boots, and a cultural afterlife as one of the internet's most enduring meme characters. The fact that it never had an official LEGO set until now is honestly a little surprising.

The LEGO Group has released two distinct products as part of this launch, designed to appeal to different types of fans. The details suggest LEGO is doing what it does best, catering to both younger builders and the adult nostalgic collector crowd who want something displayable on a bookshelf.

This is a smart move for both brands. LEGO has been aggressively expanding its licensed adult sets in recent years, banking on the fact that millennials now have disposable income and a deep appetite for nostalgia. Shrek is one of the defining cultural properties of that generation. Universal, meanwhile, has a fifth Shrek film in development, so this partnership lands at exactly the right moment to rebuild buzz around the franchise ahead of that release.
keyPoints: The LEGO Group and Universal Products and Experiences have released the first-ever official Shrek LEGO sets
The launch marks the 25th anniversary of the Shrek franchise
Two distinct products are included in the launch, aimed at different audiences
A fifth Shrek film is currently in development, making the timing of this release strategically smart
The sets tap into LEGO's growing strategy of targeting adult nostalgic collectors

articles.title: Noscroll Is an AI Bot That Doomscrolls the Internet So You Do Not Have To
articles.summary: A new AI tool called Noscroll is offering to read the internet for you, summarising what matters so you never have to fall into a doomscrolling spiral again. Noscroll positions itself as a cure for the anxiety-inducing habit of endlessly consuming bad news online. It is a clever idea that raises a real question: if an AI is doing your scrolling, are you more informed or just more removed?
articles.content:
Doomscrolling is the habit of endlessly reading negative news and social media content, often late at night, even when it makes you feel terrible. Most of us do it. Almost none of us enjoy it afterward. Noscroll wants to solve that by sending in an AI bot to do the scrolling on your behalf and then handing you only what actually matters.

The way Noscroll works is straightforward. Its AI bot crawls the internet, reads through the noise, and delivers a distilled summary of what is happening. You get the information without the algorithm-driven rabbit hole. The goal is to keep you informed without wrecking your mental state or eating three hours of your evening.

On the surface this sounds genuinely useful. The internet is designed to keep you hooked, and most news feeds are built to maximise time spent rather than understanding gained. Noscroll is essentially offering a circuit breaker. Instead of you scrolling through outrage, an AI handles the unpleasant work and passes you the highlights.

But there is a real tension here worth thinking about. Part of what makes doomscrolling feel urgent is the sensation of witnessing things yourself in real time. When an AI curates that experience for you, you are no longer a witness, you are a recipient. Noscroll gets to decide what counts as important, and that is a lot of editorial power for a bot to hold.

That said, for people who feel genuinely overwhelmed by the current information environment, Noscroll is offering something the wellness industry has been promising for years but rarely delivering: a practical, technical way out of the scroll. Whether it sticks depends on how good the summaries actually are and whether users trust Noscroll enough to stop checking for themselves.
keyPoints: Noscroll is a new AI bot that reads and summarises the internet so users avoid doomscrolling
Doomscrolling is the habit of endlessly consuming negative online content, often worsening anxiety
Noscroll's AI crawls the web and delivers only what it judges to be important
The tool raises questions about who decides what counts as significant news
It appeals directly to users who feel overwhelmed by the current online information environment

articles.title: Birkenstock, the 250-Year-Old Sandal Brand, Just Launched Nail Polish
articles.summary: Birkenstock, the German footwear brand founded in 1774, has launched its first nail polish line, moving deeper into personal care after a record-breaking revenue year. The move follows Birkenstock's growing presence on fashion runways and signals that the brand is no longer content being just a sandal company. It is a bold step for a brand that spent most of its 250 years being associated with comfort over glamour.
articles.content:
Birkenstock is not just making sandals anymore. The brand, which has been around since 1774 making it one of the oldest footwear companies in the world, has officially launched a nail polish line. Yes, nail polish. From the people who brought you the cork-footbed sandal.

This is not as random as it sounds. Birkenstock has spent the last few years aggressively repositioning itself at the intersection of comfort and high fashion. The brand has shown up on major runways, collaborated with luxury labels, and rode the so-called ugly-chic wave to record revenue. Nail polish is the next step in what Birkenstock is clearly building as a full lifestyle brand, not just a shoe company.

The personal care space is a logical place to plant a flag. Birkenstock's current customer loves the brand for its earthy, no-fuss aesthetic, and nail polish can carry that same energy. Think natural tones, clean ingredients, and packaging that looks like it belongs next to a pair of Arizonas. It extends the brand into a daily ritual without feeling like a stretch.

Birkenstock's growth story is genuinely impressive. The brand went public in October 2023, and has been posting record revenue figures as it leaned further into fashion credibility. Getting on runways and into beauty aisles at the same time is a smart double-pronged play to broaden the customer base beyond the classic sandal buyer.

The real test is whether nail polish buyers who have never owned a pair of Birkenstocks will pick up the brand through a beauty shelf discovery. If Birkenstock can pull that off, it stops being a footwear company with a lifestyle side project and starts being a genuine lifestyle brand that also happens to make very comfortable shoes.
keyPoints: Birkenstock has launched its first nail polish line, entering the personal care category
Birkenstock was founded in 1774, making it approximately 250 years old
The launch follows record revenue and increased presence on fashion runways
Birkenstock went public in October 2023 and has been growing aggressively since
The nail polish move is part of a broader strategy to become a full lifestyle brand

articles.title: The Ramones Are Celebrating 50 Years of Their Debut Album All Year Long
articles.summary: The Ramones are marking the 50th anniversary of their 1976 self-titled debut album with a full year of events, reissues, and a major museum exhibit. Rhino Records will release a series of reissues and remastered, upscaled videos, while an authorized exhibit opens at the Punk Rock Museum in Las Vegas on July 4. It is one of the biggest celebrations punk rock has seen in years.
articles.content:
Fifty years ago, four guys from Queens, New York walked into a studio and made one of the most important albums in rock history. The Ramones' 1976 debut album essentially invented American punk rock, clocking in at under 30 minutes and packed with short, fast, loud songs that changed everything. Now, in 2026, the band's estate is making sure that anniversary does not go unnoticed.

The celebration runs all year long and has two big anchors. First, Rhino Records will be rolling out a series of reissues and remastered, upscaled videos tied to the debut album. Rhino is the label division that specializes in archival and catalog releases, so fans can expect proper, high-quality treatment of the original recordings rather than a quick cash-in.

The second and arguably flashier piece is an authorized exhibit opening at the Punk Rock Museum in Las Vegas on July 4. The date is a nice touch since the Ramones were famously American to their core, with their leather jackets, ripped jeans, and New York attitude. The Punk Rock Museum in Las Vegas opened in 2023 and has quickly become the go-to destination for deep dives into punk history, so this is the right home for a Ramones retrospective.

All four original Ramones, Joey, Johnny, Dee Dee, and Tommy, have passed away, which makes a celebration like this feel both joyful and bittersweet. But their music never really went away. The debut album still sounds urgent and alive today, and a whole new generation keeps discovering it. This year-long push should make sure the next 50 years start just as loudly as the first.
keyPoints: The Ramones are celebrating the 50th anniversary of their 1976 self-titled debut album with a year-long series of events
Rhino Records will release reissues and remastered, upscaled videos from the debut album
An authorized exhibit opens at the Punk Rock Museum in Las Vegas on July 4, 2026
All four original Ramones have passed away, making this a legacy celebration by the band's estate
The Punk Rock Museum in Las Vegas opened in 2023 and is a major destination for punk history

articles.title: White Castle Is Putting Slider Vending Machines in 1,000 Hospitals and Colleges
articles.summary: White Castle is rolling out 1,000 new automated vending machines stocked with its famous sliders, targeting hospitals and college campuses across the US. It is a major expansion beyond White Castle's traditional brick-and-mortar restaurants, betting that late-night students and hungry hospital staff will happily swipe their card for a hot slider at 2am. The move puts one of America's most iconic fast food brands in places it has never been before.
articles.content:
White Castle has been making tiny square burgers since 1921, which makes it one of the oldest fast food chains in America. For most of that time, if you wanted a slider you had to go to a White Castle restaurant. That is about to change in a big way.

White Castle is placing 1,000 vending machines in hospitals and college campuses, bringing its sliders directly into spaces where people are hungry at odd hours and traditional food options are limited. A hospital worker finishing a night shift at 4am or a student pulling an all-nighter now has a genuine shot at getting a White Castle slider without leaving the building.

The machines are automated dining units rather than standard snack dispensers, meaning they are designed to serve hot food rather than just crisps and chocolate bars. White Castle has not been specific about exactly which menu items will be available in the machines, but the slider is obviously the hero product here. The chain is wagering that brand loyalty plus convenience is a powerful enough combination to make this work at scale.

For White Castle, this is a smart play. The chain has a famously passionate fanbase but its restaurant footprint is concentrated heavily in the Midwest and parts of the East Coast. Vending machines let White Castle reach new customers in new cities without the cost of opening full restaurants. If the 1,000-machine rollout performs well, there is no obvious reason it could not expand far beyond that.
keyPoints: White Castle is installing 1,000 slider vending machines in hospitals and college campuses
The machines are automated dining units designed to serve hot food
It is a major expansion beyond White Castle's traditional restaurant model
White Castle's restaurant locations are mostly concentrated in the Midwest and East Coast
The move targets high-traffic venues where food options at odd hours are limited
articles.wikiSearchQuery: White Castle fast food restaurant chain history

articles.title: Domino's Ultra Cheese Pizza Piles On Over 2 Pounds of Mozzarella
articles.summary: Domino's has unveiled its new Ultra Cheese pizza, a single pie loaded with more than 2 pounds of mozzarella on top. It is part of a wider push by Domino's to go bigger and bolder on menu items. If you have ever thought a pizza needed more cheese, Domino's is quite literally doubling down.
articles.content:
Domino's is not playing around with its latest menu drop. The Ultra Cheese pizza arrives topped with over 2 full pounds of mozzarella, which is a genuinely absurd amount of cheese on a single pizza. To put that in perspective, most standard Domino's pizzas use somewhere around 6 to 8 ounces of cheese. The Ultra Cheese is blowing past that by a factor of four or more.

Domino's is positioning the Ultra Cheese as part of a broader wave of oversized, maximalist menu items. The chain has clearly spotted an appetite for excess right now, and it is leaning into that hard. Whether this is a limited time stunt or a permanent addition, Domino's knows that "over 2 pounds of mozzarella" is the kind of detail that gets people talking, sharing, and eventually ordering.

The timing makes sense too. The fast food space is more competitive than ever, with chains constantly fighting for attention on social media. A pizza that sounds almost physically impossible to eat is exactly the kind of content that travels fast online. Domino's has played this game before, but the Ultra Cheese feels like one of the boldest swings yet.

If you are a cheese lover, this is basically your Super Bowl moment. Domino's has not officially confirmed whether the Ultra Cheese will roll out nationally or stay regional, so if you see it in your area, it might be worth grabbing while you can.
keyPoints: Domino's new Ultra Cheese pizza has over 2 pounds of mozzarella on a single pie
That is roughly four times the amount of cheese on a standard Domino's pizza
It is part of a wider Domino's push toward larger, more indulgent menu items
The launch is designed to generate buzz and social media attention
Availability details like regional vs national rollout have not been fully confirmed
